speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three. Today's date is August the 16th, 1996. And the survivor's name is Jack Gun. You were talking about staying in fields with your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Until they started to cut the harvest. I really don't know if it was-- we started in August the 23rd approximately. And I would say we were there till end of September, maybe middle September, we were laying there. And when they started to cut the harvest, we-- he advised us to go into the forests. We had a very, very thick forest not too far from Uzowa. And you know, we went from quite a long distance. And there were very, very thick woods. So we got into there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a little bit about what you mean by forest? Are they similar to the forests that we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm sure there might be some somewheres. But the forests were, you know, thick. You couldn't see inside. If you went into, you know, quite a, a distance inside, nobody could see you. And it was protection a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reason that you had gone from the field into the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they cut harvests. So there was no more wheat and no more-- we just couldn't lay in the plain fields. I mean, that would be-- we would lay there. I mean, somebody would, would, would see us in a hurry. We had to be so-- had camouflage someplace. So we went into the forest. And being in a forest, we also found some more people. In fact, those two gentlemen that were with us on that attic were there and some more people from around there-- maybe some from cities nearby. And we used to stay there all day long. And then in the evening, we used to go back to Mr. Yarushka every other evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried, you know, not to make it too obvious, you know, to go at certain times or not always, you know? But it had to be when it was dark, where we were not seen. And we used to get a little bit of food. Sometimes we'd tell them we had some more people with us. He gave us a little extra bread. And he was, in a way, he was happy when we told him we had some more people with, a little older people, you know? And he thought maybe they would, you know, help us. You know, maybe they knew, knew more about surviving or knew more about life. He he knew that we were so young. And-- When you, you went to Yarushka's at night, would, would you accompany your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. My brother never left me and took me along, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it late at night that you would go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did his-- was he married, Yarushka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was married. He had children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anyone else besides him know what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: His children knew. I'm sure his children knew what was going on. But he tried to keep it, you know, and didn't want to make it too obvious. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying when you first came that he crossed himself. Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a religious man, I believe, yes. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that had any effect on his desire to help you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so much religion. I personally feel that he's-- being so-- as I mentioned earlier, that he was very close friends with my father and knew him for so many years. I think he felt an obligation. And, and I don't fee-- I don't think it was so much that even though we had much, much goods with him and a lot of merchandise and a lot of, you know-- I personally feel that he had an ob-- he felt an obligation to help his friend's children. There was many, many people that gave lot of, lot of goods away. And then when they came for help--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians were there, what it was like, the atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, it wasn't much different as before. I mean, the Russians did not bother us. They didn't harass us. And I, I was free. And I mean, I wasn't afraid to go outside. And I did the same things as I did before they came in, like only probably difference was that I didn't have as good of food as I had before, which, you know, being young, you don't, you don't pay much attention to that. But there was no difference in my upbringing or my growing up. I mean, I didn't feel anything different from when the Russians took over from, from before, being in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after, when the Germans came in? Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, when, when the Germans came in June, they sort of, you know-- the Germans had a very good technique to demoralize the Jewish population. And this was, I think, the, the biggest fact. They were-- they put us down so much. And they-- the demoralization of the Jewish people, the, the scare that they put into us, that affected us an awful lot. And approximately two or three months after they were in, they came out with notices. And they came with loud speakers and told all the Jews to come out of their houses and take everything they can carry, their possessions, only thing that they could carry with them. And we all used to-- you had to march out and form, you know, lines. And they marched us towards one part of town, which they separated as the ghetto. And they put all the Jews from Rozyszcze into a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also remember walking with my parents and my brother and sisters, you know, with our heads down, you know, afraid to even look up, just like a herd of sheep or a herd of cattle, marching to the pasture, you know? And they marched us into that town, into to that part of the city. Usually, you know, it was the dilapidated part of town. And they put us into, into a house, like three, four families in one little house. How did you react when, when you heard this, when they are saying over the loudspeakers about how you can just take whatever you have and, and leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just frightened, fear-- didn't know what to expect. My brother tells me that I asked my parents, says, you know, I said, hey, why is the-- I, I mentioned to you I used to play with a little boy that wasn't Jewish. And I said to him, I asked my parents, I said, look, why isn't, why isn't this boy-- I don't remember his name-- why isn't he coming out? Why isn't he going with us? And the all answer would-- the only answer they could give me is because he's not Jewish. This is what they're doing to Jewish people right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you take that answer, I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how I, I took it, with a-- I didn't understand. I couldn't understand what it means. It was strange to me. I didn't know the difference about being Jewish or not being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like when you were walking towards the ghetto? Was this everyone, all the Jews from the town walking all together at once?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jews in-- from our city, walking all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if there were other people who were watching while you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I'm sure there, there were-- the, the Ukrainians and the Poles were very happy. They were [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], what they're doing to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember people standing, you know, on, on the sidewalks and watching. And some of them were yelling or talking about it or-- but I don't know what they were saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was the [INAUDIBLE] ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too far-- I would say maybe two, two, two kilometers, three kilometers. The city wasn't that big to begin with, wasn't that far of a walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the ghetto for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately a year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number one.
